notes: Expanded to 32 high-yield NCLEX drugs. Covers cardiology, anticoagulants, opioids, antibiotics, anesthesia, IV fluids, oncology, contraceptives, immunosuppressants, neurology, and OB. The seed script is idempotent (ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE) so re-running it patches existing rows.

cards.mechanism: Centrally inhibits prostaglandin synthesis; minimal anti-inflammatory effect.
cards.indications: Mild-to-moderate pain, fever reduction.
cards.contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment, hypersensitivity.
cards.black_box_warning: Hepatotoxicity with doses >4g/day or chronic alcohol use.
cards.priority_labs: LFTs (baseline + with chronic use), acetaminophen level if overdose suspected
cards.patient_teaching: Max 3-4g/day in healthy adults; max 2g/day with liver disease or chronic alcohol. Read combo product labels.
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: N-acetylcysteine (NAC). Check all OTC and combo Rx for hidden acetaminophen.

cards.mechanism: Selective beta-2 agonism relaxes bronchial smooth muscle.
cards.indications: Acute bronchospasm in asthma/COPD, exercise-induced bronchospasm.
cards.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Caution with cardiac dysrhythmias and severe HTN.
cards.priority_labs: Potassium (can drop with high doses), glucose (can rise)
cards.patient_teaching: Rescue inhaler - take 5-15 min before exercise if prescribed. Rinse mouth after. Notify provider if needing >2x/week (poor control).
cards.nursing_implications: Watch for tachycardia, tremors, restlessness. Severe asthma may need continuous neb. Spacer improves delivery.

cards.mechanism: Metabolized to 6-mercaptopurine which inhibits purine synthesis → blocks lymphocyte proliferation.
cards.indications: Transplant rejection, severe RA, inflammatory bowel disease, lupus nephritis.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy (some forms), TPMT deficiency, severe infection.
cards.black_box_warning: Increased risk of lymphoma and skin cancer.
cards.priority_labs: CBC (especially WBC), LFTs, TPMT genotype, amylase/lipase
cards.patient_teaching: Take with food to reduce GI upset. Avoid live vaccines. Use sun protection. Report sore throat, fever, easy bruising.
cards.nursing_implications: Allopurinol increases toxicity (reduce dose 75%). Monitor for myelosuppression and pancreatitis.

cards.mechanism: Sodium channel blockade reducing repetitive neuronal firing.
cards.indications: Partial seizures, trigeminal neuralgia, bipolar disorder.
cards.contraindications: Bone marrow suppression, MAOI use within 14 days, hypersensitivity to TCAs.
cards.black_box_warning: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (HLA-B*1502 in Asian ancestry), aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis.
cards.priority_labs: Carbamazepine level (4-12 mcg/mL), CBC, LFTs, sodium (SIADH), HLA-B*1502 in at-risk ethnicities
cards.patient_teaching: Take with food. Report rash, fever, easy bruising, dark urine. Avoid grapefruit. Use sunscreen.
cards.nursing_implications: Auto-induces own metabolism. Many CYP interactions. SIADH risk - monitor sodium.

cards.mechanism: Crosslinks DNA strands, inhibiting replication and triggering apoptosis.
cards.indications: Testicular, ovarian, bladder, lung, head/neck cancers.
cards.contraindications: Significant renal impairment, hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy.
cards.patient_teaching: Drink plenty of fluids. Report hearing changes, tingling in hands/feet, decreased urine.
cards.nursing_implications: Pre-hydrate with IV fluids + mannitol. Antiemetic prophylaxis (5-HT3 + dexamethasone). Monitor creatinine daily.

cards.mechanism: Inhibits calcineurin → blocks T-cell activation and IL-2 production.
cards.indications: Solid organ transplant rejection prevention, severe psoriasis, RA.
cards.contraindications: Uncontrolled HTN, malignancy, concurrent live vaccines.
cards.black_box_warning: Lymphoma and skin malignancies, severe infection, nephrotoxicity, infusion reactions.
cards.priority_labs: Cyclosporine trough level, creatinine, magnesium, potassium, BP
cards.patient_teaching: Take exactly at same times. Avoid grapefruit juice. Avoid live vaccines. Use sun protection. Report tremor, swelling, decreased urine.
cards.nursing_implications: Many drug interactions (CYP3A4). Monitor for nephrotoxicity, gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism. No grapefruit.

cards.mechanism: Inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase, increasing intracellular Ca++ and inotropy; slows AV conduction.
cards.indications: Chronic CHF, atrial fibrillation rate control.
cards.contraindications: V-fib, hypersensitivity, 2nd/3rd degree AV block without pacing.
cards.priority_labs: Digoxin level (0.5-2 ng/mL therapeutic), potassium, magnesium, creatinine
cards.patient_teaching: Take same time daily. Check pulse for 1 full minute - hold for HR<60. Report nausea, vision changes (yellow-green halos).
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: digoxin immune Fab (Digibind). Hypokalemia potentiates toxicity. Hold for apical HR<60.

cards.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase → increases ACh in brain, improving cognition.
cards.indications: Mild to severe Alzheimer's dementia.
cards.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to piperidines.
cards.patient_teaching: Take at bedtime. Modest benefit - won't reverse disease. Report fainting, abnormal heartbeat, GI bleeding.
cards.nursing_implications: Bradycardia and syncope risk. GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) common at start. Weight loss possible.

cards.mechanism: Suppresses ovulation via negative feedback on FSH/LH; thickens cervical mucus.
cards.indications: Contraception, acne, menstrual regulation.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy, smokers >35, history of VTE/stroke/MI, breast cancer, active liver disease, uncontrolled HTN.
cards.priority_labs: Blood pressure, lipid panel, liver enzymes
cards.patient_teaching: Take at same time daily. Use backup method first cycle. ACHES warning signs: Abdominal pain, Chest pain, Headaches, Eye changes, Severe leg pain.
cards.nursing_implications: Screen for smoking + clot history. No effect on STI prevention. Antibiotics may reduce effectiveness.

cards.mechanism: Blocks Na/K/2Cl cotransporter in thick ascending loop of Henle.
cards.indications: Acute pulmonary edema, CHF exacerbation, edema, hypertension, hypercalcemia.
cards.contraindications: Anuria, severe hypovolemia, sulfa allergy (relative).
cards.patient_teaching: Take in morning. Rise slowly (orthostasis). Increase K+ foods unless on K-sparing. Report cramps/weakness.
cards.nursing_implications: IV push slowly (max 20 mg/min) to avoid ototoxicity. Daily weights. Strict I&Os.

cards.mechanism: Potentiates antithrombin III; inactivates thrombin and Factor Xa.
cards.indications: DVT/PE prophylaxis and treatment, acute MI, atrial fibrillation, dialysis circuits.
cards.contraindications: Active bleeding, HIT, severe thrombocytopenia, recent neurosurgery/spinal anesthesia.
cards.black_box_warning: Spinal/epidural hematoma risk with neuraxial anesthesia; HIT can be fatal.
cards.patient_teaching: Use soft toothbrush + electric razor. Report unusual bruising, dark stools, headache.
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: protamine sulfate. Monitor for HIT after 5-10 days. Hold for invasive procedures.

cards.mechanism: Activates insulin receptor to drive glucose into cells; only insulin safe to give IV.
cards.indications: DKA, HHS, hyperkalemia (with D50), tight glycemic control in critical care.
cards.contraindications: Hypoglycemia. Use caution with renal/hepatic impairment.
cards.black_box_warning: Risk of severe hypoglycemia, especially with IV infusion.
cards.patient_teaching: Rotate injection sites. Recognize hypoglycemia (shaking, sweating, confusion). Carry fast-acting glucose.
cards.nursing_implications: Onset 30 min SC, 5 min IV. Peak 2-4 hr SC. Always verify dose with second RN. Never mix insulin types when IV.

cards.mechanism: Provides volume + electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Cl, lactate); lactate converted to bicarbonate buffers acidemia.
cards.indications: Volume resuscitation (especially burns, surgery), mild metabolic acidosis.
cards.contraindications: Liver failure (cannot metabolize lactate), severe hyperkalemia, lactic acidosis.
cards.patient_teaching: Replaces lost body fluids. Report swelling or trouble breathing.
cards.nursing_implications: Do not co-administer with ceftriaxone (calcium precipitate). Watch for fluid overload + hyperkalemia in renal failure.

cards.mechanism: Binds synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating neurotransmitter release. Mechanism partly unclear.
cards.indications: Partial-onset, myoclonic, and tonic-clonic seizures (adjunctive or monotherapy).
cards.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Caution in renal impairment.
cards.patient_teaching: Take consistently. Report behavioral changes, depression, suicidal thoughts.
cards.nursing_implications: Well tolerated overall. Watch for irritability/agitation, especially pediatrics. Adjust for renal function.

cards.mechanism: Levodopa crosses BBB and is converted to dopamine. Carbidopa blocks peripheral conversion to reduce side effects.
cards.indications: Parkinson's disease, parkinsonism symptoms.
cards.contraindications: Narrow-angle glaucoma, melanoma, MAOI within 14 days.
cards.patient_teaching: May take weeks for effect. Do not take with high-protein meals (compete for absorption). Don't stop abruptly. May cause dark sweat/urine - harmless.
cards.nursing_implications: Monitor for 'on-off' phenomenon and dyskinesias. Orthostatic hypotension common. Avoid pyridoxine without carbidopa.

cards.mechanism: Blocks fast sodium channels; shortens action potential and refractory period in ventricular tissue.
cards.indications: Ventricular dysrhythmias (V-tach with pulse, V-fib), local/regional anesthesia.
cards.contraindications: AV block (without pacing), Stokes-Adams syndrome, hypersensitivity.
cards.patient_teaching: If used for procedure, area will be numb. Report any chest pain, dizziness, or tingling around mouth.
cards.nursing_implications: Toxicity: tinnitus, perioral numbness, seizures. Cardiac monitor with IV infusion. Reduce dose in elderly and liver failure.

cards.mechanism: Blocks conversion of angiotensin I to II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone release.
cards.indications: Hypertension, CHF, post-MI, diabetic nephropathy.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy (Category D), bilateral renal artery stenosis, angioedema history.
cards.patient_teaching: Report persistent dry cough or facial/throat swelling immediately. Avoid K+ supplements/salt substitutes.
cards.nursing_implications: First-dose hypotension - monitor BP for 4 hr. Hold for SBP<90. Watch for angioedema and hyperkalemia.

cards.mechanism: CNS depressant via NMDA antagonism; smooth muscle relaxation.
cards.indications: Preeclampsia/eclampsia seizure prophylaxis, preterm labor tocolysis, torsades de pointes.
cards.contraindications: Heart block, myasthenia gravis, severe renal failure.
cards.priority_labs: Magnesium level (therapeutic 4-7 mEq/L for OB), creatinine, calcium
cards.patient_teaching: Will feel warm and flushed. Report difficulty breathing or muscle weakness.
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: calcium gluconate. Assess DTRs, RR, urine output q1h. Magnesium toxicity: loss of DTRs (first), respiratory depression, cardiac arrest.

cards.mechanism: Suppresses ovulation; thickens cervical mucus; thins endometrium.
cards.indications: Long-acting contraception, endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, breast cancer, severe liver disease.
cards.black_box_warning: Bone mineral density loss with long-term use - limit to 2 years if possible.
cards.patient_teaching: IM injection every 12 weeks. Take calcium and vitamin D. Fertility return may delay 12-18 months after discontinuation.
cards.nursing_implications: Z-track or deep IM. Do not massage site. Counsel on weight gain, irregular bleeding, mood changes.

cards.mechanism: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking DNA/RNA synthesis. Folic acid antagonist.
cards.indications: Leukemia, lymphoma, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, ectopic pregnancy.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy (Category X), severe renal/hepatic impairment, bone marrow suppression.
cards.black_box_warning: Hepatotoxicity, severe myelosuppression, pulmonary toxicity, fatal if misdosed (weekly vs daily).
cards.patient_teaching: Take EXACTLY as prescribed - typically weekly for arthritis. Reliable contraception. Report mouth sores, cough, jaundice.
cards.nursing_implications: Rescue: leucovorin (folinic acid). Verify weekly vs daily dosing with pharmacy. Monitor for stomatitis, hepatotoxicity.

cards.mechanism: Selective beta-1 adrenergic blockade lowers HR, BP, and myocardial oxygen demand.
cards.indications: Hypertension, angina, post-MI, CHF (selected), AFib rate control.
cards.contraindications: HR < 60, 2nd/3rd degree AV block, decompensated heart failure, asthma (relative).
cards.black_box_warning: Abrupt discontinuation may precipitate angina/MI in CAD patients.
cards.priority_labs: Heart rate, blood pressure, glucose (may mask hypoglycemia)
cards.patient_teaching: Don't stop abruptly. Check pulse before dose - hold if HR<60. Rise slowly.
cards.nursing_implications: Hold for HR<60 or SBP<100 unless provider says otherwise. Monitor for fatigue and depression.

cards.mechanism: Agonist at mu-opioid receptors; inhibits pain transmission and modulates perception.
cards.indications: Acute and chronic moderate-to-severe pain, MI (vasodilator effect), palliative dyspnea.
cards.contraindications: Respiratory depression, paralytic ileus, severe asthma without ventilation.
cards.black_box_warning: Respiratory depression, abuse potential, accidental overdose, neonatal opioid withdrawal.
cards.priority_labs: None routine; monitor respiratory rate + sedation scale
cards.patient_teaching: Take exactly as prescribed. Stool softener for constipation. Never mix with alcohol/benzos.
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: naloxone. Hold for RR<12. Assess pain before/after. PCA: check basal+demand limits.

cards.mechanism: Replenishes extracellular fluid volume; matches plasma tonicity.
cards.indications: Hypovolemia, hemorrhagic shock, hyponatremia, IV access maintenance.
cards.contraindications: Caution with CHF, edema, hypernatremia, severe renal impairment.
cards.patient_teaching: Will receive fluid via IV. Notify nurse for shortness of breath or swelling.
cards.nursing_implications: Monitor for fluid overload (crackles, JVD, edema). Hyperchloremic acidosis with large volumes.

cards.mechanism: Selectively blocks serotonin receptors in CTZ and vagal afferents, reducing nausea signaling.
cards.indications: Chemo-induced nausea, postop nausea, hyperemesis gravidarum.
cards.contraindications: Congenital long QT syndrome, concurrent apomorphine.
cards.patient_teaching: Dissolves on tongue (ODT form). Report irregular heartbeat or fainting.
cards.nursing_implications: Monitor QT prolongation, especially with electrolyte imbalance. Headache and constipation common.

cards.mechanism: Binds oxytocin receptors on uterine smooth muscle, stimulating contractions.
cards.indications: Labor induction/augmentation, postpartum hemorrhage prevention/treatment.
cards.contraindications: Fetal distress, cephalopelvic disproportion, prior classic C-section, placenta previa.
cards.black_box_warning: Uterine rupture, fetal/maternal harm with improper use.
cards.patient_teaching: Will cause stronger contractions. Continuous fetal monitoring required.
cards.nursing_implications: Piggyback into mainline. Titrate by uterine response. Hold for tachysystole (>5 contractions in 10 min) or non-reassuring FHR.

cards.mechanism: Stabilizes neuronal membranes by inhibiting sodium influx, raising seizure threshold.
cards.indications: Tonic-clonic and partial seizures, status epilepticus, trigeminal neuralgia.
cards.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia, AV block, porphyria.
cards.black_box_warning: IV cardiovascular collapse if infused too rapidly.
cards.priority_labs: Phenytoin level (10-20 mcg/mL), albumin, CBC, LFTs
cards.patient_teaching: Take same time daily. Good oral hygiene (gingival hyperplasia). Avoid alcohol. Don't stop abruptly. Stevens-Johnson rash = emergency.
cards.nursing_implications: IV: max 50 mg/min, dedicated line with saline (precipitates with dextrose). Many drug interactions. Free vs total level in low albumin.

cards.mechanism: GABA-A receptor agonist; rapid loss of consciousness via global CNS depression.
cards.indications: Induction/maintenance of general anesthesia, ICU sedation.
cards.contraindications: Egg, soybean, or sulfite allergy. Use caution in hypotension and cardiac instability.
cards.priority_labs: Triglycerides (with prolonged infusion), CPK, lactate, ABG
cards.patient_teaching: Will fall asleep quickly. Inform staff about egg/soy allergies. May feel groggy on wakeup.
cards.nursing_implications: Dedicated IV line. Monitor for hypotension, bradycardia, and propofol infusion syndrome. Strict aseptic technique.

cards.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake at the presynaptic neuronal membrane.
cards.indications: Major depressive disorder, panic, OCD, PTSD, social anxiety, PMDD.
cards.contraindications: MAOI use within 14 days, pimozide use.
cards.black_box_warning: Suicidality in children/adolescents/young adults; serotonin syndrome.
cards.patient_teaching: Takes 4-6 weeks for full effect. Don't stop abruptly. Report mood worsening or suicidal thoughts. No alcohol.
cards.nursing_implications: Monitor for serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, agitation, tremor, hyperreflexia). Suicide assessment, especially first 4 weeks.

cards.mechanism: Acts as acetylcholine, prolonged depolarization of motor end-plate causes paralysis.
cards.indications: Rapid sequence intubation, short procedures requiring muscle relaxation.
cards.contraindications: Personal/family history of malignant hyperthermia, hyperkalemia, burns >24h, recent stroke or spinal cord injury.
cards.black_box_warning: Cardiac arrest in pediatric patients with undiagnosed myopathy; risk of malignant hyperthermia.
cards.patient_teaching: Used during intubation - you will not remember it.
cards.nursing_implications: Have dantrolene ready for malignant hyperthermia. Monitor train-of-four. Brief duration (5-10 min).

cards.mechanism: Binds FKBP-12 → inhibits calcineurin → blocks T-cell activation.
cards.indications: Transplant rejection prevention (kidney, liver, heart), refractory autoimmune disease.
cards.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to macrolides or polyoxyl 60 hydrogenated castor oil.
cards.black_box_warning: Lymphoma + skin malignancies, severe infection, nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity.
cards.priority_labs: Tacrolimus trough, creatinine, potassium, glucose, magnesium
cards.patient_teaching: Same times daily, with or without food but be consistent. Avoid grapefruit. Use contraception. Report tremor, headache, weight gain.
cards.nursing_implications: Narrow therapeutic index. Monitor for new-onset diabetes, hypertension, neurotoxicity. No grapefruit/St. John's wort.

cards.mechanism: Inhibits gram-positive cell wall synthesis. Used for MRSA, C. diff (oral).
cards.indications: MRSA infections, severe gram-positive infections, C. difficile colitis (PO).
cards.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Renal impairment requires dosing adjustment.
cards.priority_labs: Trough levels (15-20 mcg/mL for serious infections), creatinine, hearing baseline
cards.patient_teaching: Report changes in hearing, ringing in ears, decreased urine output.
cards.nursing_implications: Infuse over at least 60 min to prevent vancomycin flushing syndrome (red man). Monitor for nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.

cards.mechanism: Inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) and proteins C and S.
cards.indications: Long-term anticoagulation for AFib, mechanical valves, recurrent VTE.
cards.contraindications: Pregnancy (Category X), active bleeding, severe liver disease.
cards.priority_labs: PT/INR (target 2-3 for most indications, 2.5-3.5 mechanical valves)
cards.patient_teaching: Maintain consistent vitamin K intake (greens). Avoid NSAIDs, ASA without provider OK. Carry medical ID.
cards.nursing_implications: Antidote: vitamin K (slow), FFP/PCC (urgent). Bridge with heparin/LMWH at initiation.
